EPISODE_NARRATIVE: A slow moving front produced heavy rain over Southwest Washington that resulted in the flooding of the Gray's River near Rosburg.
EVENT_NARRATIVE: The Grays River near Rosburg reached the flood stage of 12 feet at 715 AM PST on December 20th. The river crested at 13.6 feet at 12:15 AM PST on December 21 before falling below flood stage at 8:15 AM PST.

EPISODE_NARRATIVE: A weak shortwave passing through an environment characterized by moderate CAPE and low shear resulted in an isolated severe thunderstorm over the Upper Columbia Basin.  The storm developed over Davenport, WA producing one inch hail briefly before collapsing.
EVENT_NARRATIVE: One inch hail reported by trained spotter in Davenport, WA.

EPISODE_NARRATIVE: A downed power line was thought to be responsible for a twenty acre brush fire north of Deer Park near Eloika Lake.  It was not clear what caused the power line to go down.  Multiple outbuildings and vehicles were burned in the blaze.
EVENT_NARRATIVE: A 20 acre brush fire burned five outbuildings, a camper trailer, a horse trailer, an RV, and a car.

EPISODE_NARRATIVE: A strong upper-level ridge during the end of July and beginning August helped fuel a  moist and unstable air mass over the region.  The ridge broke down on the 1st of August and was overrun by two weak midlevel impulses on August 2nd.  As the second impulse passed over Central Washington during the late afternoon, strong to severe storms developed over the Waterville Plateau producing a combination of heavy rain and moderate size hail.  Meanwhile, dewpoint depressions over Eastern Washington had grown between 40 and 50 degrees Fahrenheit.  The rain and hail cooled outflow winds from storms over the Waterville Plateau spread east and gained momentum while tracking along the Highway 2 corridor across the Upper Columbia Basin.  Additional storms fired along the gust front and continued to reinforce the cold pool and resultant winds.  Gusty winds and blowing dust which originated over the Waterville Plateau traveled across Banks Lake and through much of Lincoln County but generally remained below severe speeds.  It wasn't until the outflow boundary moved into Eastern Lincoln County and Lake Roosevelt near Seven Bays Marina when wind speeds ranged between 60 and 70 miles per hour.  The winds led to numerous downed trees, waves four to six feet on Lake Roosevelt, widespread boat damage, power outages, and structural damage.  The winds also fanned new wildfire starts from lightning.  As the afternoon progressed, a combination of forcing from the shortwave and gust front continued to fuel additional storms over Northeastern Washington.   Outflow winds from these storms generally moved north to south.  As the north to south boundaries interacted with the westerly boundaries, some locations were spared experiencing wind gusts between 35 and 45 mph.  In this absence, winds of 50 to 70 mph were observed coupled with widespread tree damage.  A few storms briefly produced quarter size hail between Davenport and Northwest Spokane.
EVENT_NARRATIVE: The Midnight Mine RAWS measured a wind gust of 68 mph.  Numerous boats were damaged on Lake Roosevelt due to the winds.  Boaters and videos indicated wind waves of four to six feet on the Lake.  At least ten boats were severely damaged, two boats capsized, and numerous other boats received minor damage.  Several docks were damaged.  No injuries were reported.  Numerous trees were downed across southern Stevens County.

EPISODE_NARRATIVE: The tornado destroyed a carport at one house. The trestles were also cracked inside of the garage. A utility pole was bent back (leaning) which was being supported by a digger. The garbage can was found in the trees; the trees were damaged. Several shingles were ripped from roofs.  A road sign and several more trees were also damaged nearby.  At the local Eagle's Club, siding was peeled back on one corner with branches strewn across the property.
EVENT_NARRATIVE: The tornado destroyed a carport at one house. The trestles were also cracked inside of the garage. A utility pole was bent back (leaning) which was being supported by a digger. The garbage can was found in the trees; the trees were damaged. Several shingles were ripped from roofs.  A road sign and several more trees were also damaged nearby.  At the local Eagle's Club, siding was peeled back on one corner with branches strewn across the property.  Estimated peak wind speeds of 75 mph.

EPISODE_NARRATIVE: A deepening shortwave trough of low pressure spun up a low pressure system at the surface.  This low pressure system swung through a strong warm front that tapped into an atmospheric river with sub-tropic origins out of the Pacific Ocean.  There were two regions in eastern Washington that experienced heavy snowfall with most other areas seeing rain or light snowfall before transitioning over to rain.  The first area to receive heavy snowfall was in the lee of the northern Cascade Mountains.  Thermal profiles during the event for this area supported snow due to cold air damning.  Strong warm air advection across the basin resulted in mostly rain with heavy snow observed further north where temperatures were colder across the Spokane area and northeast mountains of Washington.
EVENT_NARRATIVE: Widespread storm total snowfall totals of between three and seven inches.

EPISODE_NARRATIVE: A moist warm front overrunning an established pool of cold air over Eastern Washington resulted in several hours of light to moderate snowfall on the 10th of February.  The heaviest snow accumulations were found in the East Slopes of the Washington Cascades which received between seven to fourteen inches.  The remainder of the region saw snowfall amounts generally under two inches.  The exception was along a narrow corridor across Northeastern Washington, generally south of Pend Oreille River and north of the Palouse, extending east to west from Lincoln County to the Idaho Montana border.  A nearly stationary boundary coupled with the rising terrain resulted in several hours of moderate snow which accumulated between three and four inches over a span of just a few hours.
EVENT_NARRATIVE: Snowfall amounts generally ranged from one to three inches.  An isolated report of four inches was received from the town of Wauconda.

EPISODE_NARRATIVE: Periods of moderate to heavy snow continued for the fifth straight day across the East Slopes of the Washington Cascades.  The persistent westerly jet feeding subtropical moisture into the region was temporarily disrupted as a trough dropped out of the Gulf of Alaska and passed through the region.  Instability within the trough coupled with strong westerly flow promoted consistent orographic showers along the crest which extended well into the upper valleys.  This precipitation regime was gradually replaced with stable snowfall as trough tracked inland and the next plume of deep moisture and associated warm front lifted into the region on the 17th.  Over a foot of snow fell along and adjacent to the crest impacting the communities of Plain, Holden Village, and Mazama.  Snowfall amounts near six inches were common away from the crest in locations like Leavenworth.
EVENT_NARRATIVE: Rising snow levels led to heavy snow in the mountains and little to no accumulations in the valleys.  Ski resorts in the area reported eight to twelve inches of snow.

EVENT_NARRATIVE: Heavy snow fell above elevations of 2000 feet.  Snowfall amounts ranged from six inches near Leavenworth to sixteen inches in Holden Village.  Plain picked up between twelve and fourteen inches.  Six to ten inches fell in the upper reaches of the Methow Valley.

EPISODE_NARRATIVE: A deep fetch of Pacific moisture brought heavy snow to the mountains and moderate to heavy rain to the valleys across the area overnight on January 10th into the 11th.  Localized flooding was observed in the northeast mountains of Washington due to a combination of rainfall, snow melt and frozen soils.  Rainfall amounts were generally up into the half inch to one inch range for this period, which typically would not cause flooding if not in combination with low elevation snow melt and frozen soils preventing water from seeping into the ground.  Flooding impacts were minor and limited to low spots in the terrain, in urban areas and along small streams.  Rain and heavy snow was followed by a strong cold front passage during the early morning hours of January 11th with strong and damaging winds observed across the much of the area.
EVENT_NARRATIVE: A ten inch diameter tree snapped off in the Five Mile Prairie area north of Spokane; some houses also lost shingles in the area.  There was also a report of an old growth tree that lost four large branches with a girth of eight inches in diameter north of Davenport.  One of these branches flew about ten feet away from the tree.

EPISODE_NARRATIVE: A strong westerly jet aimed directly at the Pacific Northwest brought several rounds of moderate to heavy snow to the East Slopes of the Washington Cascades.  The accompanying moisture fetch had origins to the tropics however adequate cool air damming east of the crest allowed precipitation to remain mainly in the form of snow.  Snow to liquid ratios indicated it was a very wet snow.  Snow accumulations were generally in the seven to ten inch range along the East Slopes of the Cascades and three to five inches across the Wenatchee Area and Waterville Plateau.
EVENT_NARRATIVE: Snowfall amounts ranged between three and four inches with local amounts of five inches reported in Waterville.

EPISODE_NARRATIVE: This period was dominated by a strong upper level ridge of high pressure.  Subsidence associated with this ridge trapped low level moisture with widespread fog developing across the area.  Freezing temperatures and dense fog produced some rime on power lines leading to power outages across the area.
EVENT_NARRATIVE: The Bonneville Power Administration reported issues of riming on their transmission lines running from the Mead area (Bell substation) out onto the west plains.  Avista Utility also reported riming on their transmission lines with power outages reported across the Spokane area.  This included a broken conductor and broken poles between the Four Mounds area to the Idaho state border.

EPISODE_NARRATIVE: A moist warm front overrunning an established pool of cold air over Eastern Washington resulted in several hours of light to moderate snowfall on the 10th of February.  The heaviest snow accumulations were found in the East Slopes of the Washington Cascades which received between seven to fourteen inches.  The remainder of the region saw snowfall amounts generally under two inches.  The exception was along a narrow corridor across Northeastern Washington, generally south of Pend Oreille River and north of the Palouse, extending east to west from Lincoln County to the Idaho Montana border.  A nearly stationary boundary coupled with the rising terrain resulted in several hours of moderate snow which accumulated between three and four inches over a span of just a few hours.
EVENT_NARRATIVE: Light to moderate snow fell across the East Slopes during the morning hours of the 10th ahead of a strong cold front.  Behind the cold front, an unstable air mass and strong westerly flow resulted in numerous convective snow showers along and east of the Cascade Crest.  The morning snow brought between two and four inches then an additional four to eight inches fell at some locations with the post frontal showers.  Locations near the Cascade Crest including Mazama, Holden Village, and Stevens Pass received between nine and thirteen inches of snow from the event.  Further east away from the crest, snowfall amounts were generally in the six to nine inch range.

EPISODE_NARRATIVE: A moist Pacific storm system pushing into the region off of the Pacific brought heavy snow across the mountains of northeast Washington.  Strong low level warm air advection limited heavy snowfall above 4,000 feet in elevation.
EVENT_NARRATIVE: Storm total snowfall totals of nine and ten inches at 49 Degrees North Ski Resort and Mt. Spokane Ski Resort respectively.

EPISODE_NARRATIVE: A shortwave trough riding along a surface occluded front brought a burst of strong winds to the Beaches and Headlands along the Northern and Central Oregon coast.
EVENT_NARRATIVE: Two stations near the beaches of the Southwest Washington Coast, Cape Disappointment and Toke Point measured sustained winds between 40 and 45 mph from the afternoon of December 8th through the morning of December 9th. Cape Disappointment also measured a peak gust of 56 mph.

EPISODE_NARRATIVE: A deep fetch of Pacific moisture brought heavy snow to the mountains and moderate to heavy rain to the valleys across the area overnight on January 10th into the 11th.  Localized flooding was observed in the northeast mountains of Washington due to a combination of rainfall, snow melt and frozen soils.  Rainfall amounts were generally up into the half inch to one inch range for this period, which typically would not cause flooding if not in combination with low elevation snow melt and frozen soils preventing water from seeping into the ground.  Flooding impacts were minor and limited to low spots in the terrain, in urban areas and along small streams.  Rain and heavy snow was followed by a strong cold front passage during the early morning hours of January 11th with strong and damaging winds observed across the much of the area.
EVENT_NARRATIVE: Winds snapped off the top of some trees.  Damage cost was estimated.

EPISODE_NARRATIVE: A 974 millibar surface low moved northward paralleling the Oregon coast around 80 miles offshore, and resulted in one of the strongest wind storms that Northwest Oregon had experienced in past several of years. The strongest winds occurred as high pressure filled in behind the low. Southwest Washington was less affected than Northwest Oregon, but still saw its fair share of gusty winds along the coast and the Vancouver metro area.
EVENT_NARRATIVE: A Department of Transportation sensor measure sustained winds of 40 mph.

EPISODE_NARRATIVE: A strong westerly jet aimed directly at the Pacific Northwest brought several rounds of moderate to heavy snow to the East Slopes of the Washington Cascades.  The accompanying moisture fetch had origins to the tropics however adequate cool air damming east of the crest allowed precipitation to remain mainly in the form of snow.  Snow to liquid ratios indicated it was a very wet snow.  Snow accumulations were generally in the seven to ten inch range along the East Slopes of the Cascades and three to five inches across the Wenatchee Area and Waterville Plateau.
EVENT_NARRATIVE: Steady light to moderate snow fell across the East Slopes through the evening hours of the 15th then snow transitioned toward convective post frontal showers with the heaviest activity focusing near the Cascade Crest.  Snowfall amounts generally ranged from six to eight inches with the highest amounts near the crest.

EPISODE_NARRATIVE: A house along the private sidewalk Brighton Boardwalk in Clinton was destroyed by a Sunday afternoon mudslide.
EVENT_NARRATIVE: A house along the private sidewalk Brighton Boardwalk in Clinton was destroyed by a Sunday afternoon mudslide.  The house next door was also damaged.

EPISODE_NARRATIVE: Flooding near Brinnon on the Duckabush river caused damage. ||Both Fire District 2 (Quilcene Fire Rescue) and Fire District 4 (Brinnon Fire Department) conducted multiple rescues of flood-stranded residents and carried out emergency sandbagging operations at several locations. At one point a joint agency swift water rescue team was dispatched to assist with the rescue of a family trapped in rising waters inside their home.
EVENT_NARRATIVE: Fire District 4 suffered major flood damage to Station 42, near the Duckabush River. The station is reported to be a total loss (at one point fire personnel measured more than 5 feet of water in the station ��� with fire apparatus still inside) The loss of the station and equipment is estimated at nearly $200,000. About 6 homes in the area suffered damage.

EPISODE_NARRATIVE: A deep fetch of Pacific moisture brought heavy snow to the mountains and moderate to heavy rain to the valleys across the area overnight on January 10th into the 11th.  Localized flooding was observed in the northeast mountains of Washington due to a combination of rainfall, snow melt and frozen soils.  Rainfall amounts were generally up into the half inch to one inch range for this period, which typically would not cause flooding if not in combination with low elevation snow melt and frozen soils preventing water from seeping into the ground.  Flooding impacts were minor and limited to low spots in the terrain, in urban areas and along small streams.  Rain and heavy snow was followed by a strong cold front passage during the early morning hours of January 11th with strong and damaging winds observed across the much of the area.
EVENT_NARRATIVE: Minor urban flooding reported in and around Deer Park.  Residence living on 2nd Avenue indicated water over the roadway and some water seeping into a basement of a building.  Large puddles covering other roads around town, but all were passable without any closures reported.  Damage costs were estimated.

EVENT_NARRATIVE: A 40 foot cell phone tower collapsed due to high winds at 49 Degrees North Ski Area.  The cell tower smashed through the rough of an adjacent building that contained some transmission equipment.  A tree also was knocked over into some power lines near the resort at 0948PST; power was later restored at 1135PST.

EVENT_NARRATIVE: A portion of Highway 24 was closed due to an overabundance of wind-blown tumble weeds onto the highway.  Snow plows had to be called out to remove the tumble weeds before the highway could be reopened.

EPISODE_NARRATIVE: A deepening shortwave trough of low pressure spun up a low pressure system at the surface.  This low pressure system swung through a strong warm front that tapped into an atmospheric river with sub-tropic origins out of the Pacific Ocean.  There were two regions in eastern Washington that experienced heavy snowfall with most other areas seeing rain or light snowfall before transitioning over to rain.  The first area to receive heavy snowfall was in the lee of the northern Cascade Mountains.  Thermal profiles during the event for this area supported snow due to cold air damning.  Strong warm air advection across the basin resulted in mostly rain with heavy snow observed further north where temperatures were colder across the Spokane area and northeast mountains of Washington.
EVENT_NARRATIVE: Widespread storm total snowfall amounts of two to five inches reported.

EPISODE_NARRATIVE: An atmospheric river overrunning a dome of cold air established over Eastern Washington resulted in an extended period of moderate to heavy precipitation across the region.  Precipitation mainly fell in the form of snow for the East Slopes of the Cascades and northern mountains resulting in snow accumulations of seven to fourteen inches.  In the northern valleys, snow gradually transitioned to rain after two to four inches of snow fell.  A few locations experienced a period of sleet and freezing rain as this transition occurred.
EVENT_NARRATIVE: Light to moderate snow fell across the East Slopes during the evening and overnight hours of the 11th resulting in four to six inches for most locations.  Post frontal convective snow showers slopping across the Cascade Crest brought an additional two to four inches of snow to locations near the Cascade Crest including Mazama, Holden Village, and Stevens Pass.  These locations received between seven and eleven inches of snow throughout the event leading to hazardous travel, especially across the mountain passes.

EVENT_NARRATIVE: The only high wind reports we received were from Enumclaw.  A spotter reported 40g60 mph winds at 555 AM Tue Nov 11.  Later that night around 10 PM two automated stations reported 58-61 mph gusts around Enumclaw.  Elsewhere, where advisories were in effect, advisory-strength winds were reported at times.  South King, Pierce, and Kitsap counties had winds mostly in the 20g35 mph range--sub-advisory.  Power outages to nearly 200,000 customers was estimated to cost Puget Sound Energy about 4 million dollars.

EPISODE_NARRATIVE: Cold air plunged south out of the Canadian Rockies and created an east wind and down-slope wind storm across Southwest Washington. Eastern Clark County was hit the hardest with more than 60,000 customers losing power and several homes damaged by downed trees.
EVENT_NARRATIVE: Winds in the Vancouver metro area gusted 45 to 60 mph for around 10 hours. Several trees and power lines were downed by high winds near Arnada Park, three homes were damaged by downed trees in Camas, and a 18 to 20 inch diameter tree snapped near the base in Battleground. A NWS employee in the eastern suburbs of Vancouver recorded a 55 mph wind gust.

EPISODE_NARRATIVE: An atmospheric river overrunning a dome of cold air established over Eastern Washington resulted in an extended period of moderate to heavy precipitation across the region.  Precipitation mainly fell in the form of snow for the East Slopes of the Cascades and northern mountains resulting in snow accumulations of seven to fourteen inches.  In the northern valleys, snow gradually transitioned to rain after two to four inches of snow fell.  A few locations experienced a period of sleet and freezing rain as this transition occurred.
EVENT_NARRATIVE: Two to four inches of snow fell in the valleys before snow levels climbed near 3000 feet.  In the mountains, seven to fourteen inches of snow was observed.

EPISODE_NARRATIVE: A strong westerly jet aimed directly at the Pacific Northwest brought several rounds of moderate to heavy snow to the East Slopes of the Washington Cascades. The accompanying moisture fetch could be traced back to the tropics however adequate cool air damming east of the crest allowed precipitation to remain mainly in the form of snow. Snow to liquid ratios indicated it was a very wet snow. Snow accumulations were generally in the six to ten inch range.
EVENT_NARRATIVE: Light to moderate snow fell across the East Slopes during the early morning hours of the 14th resulting in four to six inches for most locations. Post frontal convective snow showers slopping across the Cascade Crest brought an additional one to three inches of snow to locations near the Cascade Crest including Mazama, Holden Village, and Stevens Pass. A COOP in Mazama reported ten inches from the event.  This led to hazardous travel across the mountain passes.

EPISODE_NARRATIVE: A 974 millibar surface low moved northward paralleling the Oregon coast around 80 miles offshore, and resulted in one of the strongest wind storms that Northwest Oregon had experienced in past several of years. The strongest winds occurred as high pressure filled in behind the low. Southwest Washington was less affected than Northwest Oregon, but still saw its fair share of gusty winds along the coast and the Vancouver metro area.
EVENT_NARRATIVE: Two Weather Stations along the Southwest Washington Coast measured wind gusts around 63 mph for a couple of hours in the evening.

EVENT_NARRATIVE: Two Weather observers reported measured wind gusts around 58 mph in Vancouver and a peak gust of 60 mph was reported on the Glen Jackson Bridge on Interstate 205. This accident resulted in traffic delays on Interstate 205 and closed boat traffic for just over an hour. Additional winds on January 24th resulted in the collapse of a carport at a Vancouver apartment complex. Over 2000 Clark Power Utility customers lost power for around an hour due to downed tree limbs on power lines.

EPISODE_NARRATIVE: Thunderstorms near the Spokane Stevens County border collapsed on the afternoon off the May 5th sending gusty north outflow winds of 30 to 45 mph through Medical Lake, WA.  The winds resulted in localized roof damage to a small apartment building.
EVENT_NARRATIVE: A gust front with winds estimated between 30 and 45 mph tore off a secondary metal rood from a one story apartment building located near the intersection of W Manilla and E Espanola Rd.  A section of roof blew into the powerlines resulting in power outages along the street.  No damage was observed to adjacent structures or vegetation.

EPISODE_NARRATIVE: Scattered thunderstorms developed across Northeastern Washington during the afternoon of June 3rd in an environment characterized by weak shear and moderate instability.  A weak midlevel dropping south out of British Columbia enhanced lift during the afternoon hours.  Two storms briefly became very strong to severe producing hail in excess of one inch.
EVENT_NARRATIVE: Public reported golf ball size hail.

EPISODE_NARRATIVE: A moist and unstable atmosphere over Eastern Washington coupled with weak shear led to widely scattered thunderstorms on the afternoon of June 26th.  A cluster of storms moved through downtown Spokane depositing upwards of an inch of rainfall in less than an hour.  The city's storm drains were overwhelmed and several businesses near the intersection of 2nd and Howard Street were flooded.
EVENT_NARRATIVE: Several businesses along Howard Street near the intersection of 2nd Avenue were flooded when heavy rain overwhelmed the city's storm drains.  Water was spilling back into buildings through toilets and drain pipes.  Among the businesses were The Interplayers Theatre and Howard Street Boxing.

EPISODE_NARRATIVE: A cold front brought strong winds to Southwest Washington March 5, 2014 during the evening. The associated deep surface low moved inland into Vancouver Island early the next morning (March 6th) dragging a bent-back occluded front across Southwest Washington, and producing another round of strong winds.
EVENT_NARRATIVE: An Oregon Department of Transportation weather station on Megler Bridge reported high winds for 17 hours with a maximum sustained winds of 50 mph and a peak gust of 72 mph. Another station at Cape Disappointment measured high winds for around 12 hours with maximum sustained winds of 48 mph and a peak gust of 62 mph.

EPISODE_NARRATIVE: A cold front brought strong winds to Southwest Washington March 5, 2014 during the evening. The associated deep surface low moved inland into Vancouver Island early the next morning (March 6th) dragging a bent-back occluded front across Southwest Washington, and producing another round of strong winds.
EVENT_NARRATIVE: The Abernathy RAWS (ABNW1) measured high winds for 2 hours early in the aftrnoon on March 6,2014 with a peak gust of 62 mph.

EPISODE_NARRATIVE: A moist front produced heavy rain in Southwest Washington that resulted in brief flooding of the grays River near Rosburg.
EVENT_NARRATIVE: Heavy rain resulted in the Grays River near Rosburg to reach flood stage around 7 pm on March 8. The river crested at 15 feet around 1 am on March 9th and fell below flood stage around 6 pm.

EPISODE_NARRATIVE: A lightning caused fire burned over 12,000 acres for much of the month of August.  As of August 27th it was in the clean up phase and was 100% contained.
EVENT_NARRATIVE: Inciweb.

EPISODE_NARRATIVE: Thunderstorms that developed just west of the Oregon Cascades produced an outflow boundary with moderate wind speeds.  Winds were strong enough to produce blowing dust across the I-82 corridor, reducing visibility to less than 100 ft.  This caused multiple collisions involving roughly 50 vehicles. No Fatalities but 9 people were hospitalized with minor injuries.
EVENT_NARRATIVE: Tri-City Herald.

EPISODE_NARRATIVE: East winds brought very cold air from east of the Cascades through the Columbia River Gorge as a moist front pushed in from the Pacific. The combination of the cold air mass and frontal precipitation resulted in snow and ice for the Gorge and the South Washington Cascades.
EVENT_NARRATIVE: Around 0.5 to 0.75 inch of ice accumulated in the Western Columbia River Gorge resulting in numerous reports of accidents due to icy roads on Interstate 84. A Spotter reported .25 inch in Stevenson and a local media meteorologist reported 0.75 inch in Corbett.

EPISODE_NARRATIVE: The passage of an upper-level trough provided the lift and instability necessary for strong thunderstorms near the Blue Mountains.  The storms produced strong winds and large hail at times.  One storm tracked through Asotin County and through the community of Anatone.
EVENT_NARRATIVE: Trained spotter estimated thunderstorm winds of 55 to 60 mph in Anatone near the intersection of Highway 129 and Pine Street.  Tree limbs branches were down across town.  Hail to the size of pennies was also observed.  Larger hail likely accompanied the storms prior to moving into Anatone.

EPISODE_NARRATIVE: Several weeks of warm and dry weather coupled with ongoing drought conditions from the previous winter resulted in very dry fuels around the Wenatchee Area.  Gusty winds on the evening of the 8th helped fan a grass fire some time after 2300 PDT near Chelan, WA.  The fire was small and contained after a few hours but managed to burn a motor home and farm tractor.  The cause of the fire is unknown.
EVENT_NARRATIVE: A grass fire sometime after 2300 PDT Sunday evening burned a motor home and farm tractor on Wilmorth Road off Highway 97 east of Chelan, WA.

EPISODE_NARRATIVE: The first autumn like cold front pushed through the region during the evening hours of October 25th bringing several hours of strong winds.  The winds snapped a large area of a tree off in the Spokane Area which fell and damaged a car parked underneath it.  Several power outages were noted around the region but this was the only damage contributed to the wind.  Sustained winds were between 25 and 35 mile per hour with gusts between 45 and 55 miles per hour.
EVENT_NARRATIVE: Video from local media showed a large section of a tree snapped from strong winds.  The branch was around a foot in diameter and damaged a parked car.  The incident took place near the intersection of East 12th Avenue and South Pittsburg Street.

EPISODE_NARRATIVE: A closed upper-level low over the Washington Cascades delivered a low shear but unstable environment.  Sufficient atmospheric moisture, characterized by precipitable water values near one inch, was already in place.  A combination of afternoon heating and lift along spokes of energy pivoting around the main low sparked numerous rounds of wet thunderstorms between the early morning hours of August 13th through morning of the 15th.  The heaviest storms were delivering around one inch of precipitation over a thirty to forty minute time period.  Several debris flows were observed in areas where the storms moved over burn scars.
EVENT_NARRATIVE: Debris flowed onto Highway 153 out of small drainages between Black Canyon Road and Squaw Creek Road.  Squaw Creek Road washed out approximately five miles west of Highway 153.    The entire area was part of the Carlton Complex burn scar.

EPISODE_NARRATIVE: A group of eight people were caught in a rip current near Ocean Shores.  They all eventually escaped to shore, but one person went back in to the water to help others.  He was then swept out to sea and became missing, presumed drowned.
EVENT_NARRATIVE: A group of eight people were caught in a rip current near Ocean Shores.  All escaped to shore but one went back in to help others, was swept out to sea, became missing after a US Coast Guard and other authority search, and presumed dead.

EVENT_NARRATIVE: Second hand report passed on by the USFS of a debris flow near the Hansel Creek Wildfire burn scar.  No additional information was received.

EVENT_NARRATIVE: Numerous spots on Highway 150 between Chelan and Manson experienced flooding from heavy rainfall.  Water got into some homes.  Rainfall amounts ranged between one inch and one and a half inches.

EPISODE_NARRATIVE: A strong upper-level ridge during the end of July and beginning August helped fuel a  moist and unstable air mass over the region.  The ridge broke down on the 1st of August and was overrun by two weak midlevel impulses on August 2nd.  As the second impulse passed over Central Washington during the late afternoon, strong to severe storms developed over the Waterville Plateau producing a combination of heavy rain and moderate size hail.  Meanwhile, dewpoint depressions over Eastern Washington had grown between 40 and 50 degrees Fahrenheit.  The rain and hail cooled outflow winds from storms over the Waterville Plateau spread east and gained momentum while tracking along the Highway 2 corridor across the Upper Columbia Basin.  Additional storms fired along the gust front and continued to reinforce the cold pool and resultant winds.  Gusty winds and blowing dust which originated over the Waterville Plateau traveled across Banks Lake and through much of Lincoln County but generally remained below severe speeds.  It wasn't until the outflow boundary moved into Eastern Lincoln County and Lake Roosevelt near Seven Bays Marina when wind speeds ranged between 60 and 70 miles per hour.  The winds led to numerous downed trees, waves four to six feet on Lake Roosevelt, widespread boat damage, power outages, and structural damage.  The winds also fanned new wildfire starts from lightning.  As the afternoon progressed, a combination of forcing from the shortwave and gust front continued to fuel additional storms over Northeastern Washington.   Outflow winds from these storms generally moved north to south.  As the north to south boundaries interacted with the westerly boundaries, some locations were spared experiencing wind gusts between 35 and 45 mph.  In this absence, winds of 50 to 70 mph were observed coupled with widespread tree damage.  A few storms briefly produced quarter size hail between Davenport and Northwest Spokane.
EVENT_NARRATIVE: A thunderstorm that passed over the Methow Valley produced wind gusts that knocked down power lines and toppled trees.  Several roads were temporarily closed from downed trees including Highway 20, Highway 153, and Twin Lakes Road.  Several residences in Twisp and Winthrop area were damaged when trees hit the homes or landed on roofs.  Lightning also sparked new wildfires within the Methow Valley, one which grew large and became known as the Little Bridge Creek Wildfire.

EPISODE_NARRATIVE: The Devil's Elbow Complex was four wildfires located on the Colville Indian Reservation in Ferry and Okanogan County.  The fire burned 26,000 acres through very steep and rocky terrain.  Three fires were in the San Poil River Valley approximately ten miles north of Keller.  The fourth fire was twenty miles north of Nespelem.
EVENT_NARRATIVE: The Devils Elbow consisted of four fires.  Three of the fires were located in Ferry County in the Okanogan Highlands.  The fourth fire which was 250 acres was located twenty miles north of Nespelem in Okanogan County.  The fire largely burned through timber, grass, brush, litter, and heavy slash that resulted from a local wind storm in 2012.  Approximately 150 homes and 90 other structures were threatened by this fire and Highway 21 was closed for over a week.

EPISODE_NARRATIVE: Storms moving through Western Washington brought lightning, heavy rain and hail. Lightning hit trees in the Fremont and Green Lake neighborhoods, in one case sparking a garage fire.  Power was knocked out in northeast Seattle and crews worked to replace 24 transformers damaged by lightning.
EVENT_NARRATIVE: Storms moving through Western Washington brought lightning, heavy rain and hail. Lightning hit trees in the Fremont and Green Lake neighborhoods, in one case sparking a garage fire.  Power was knocked out to about 2000 customers in northeast Seattle and crews worked to replace 24 transformers damaged by lightning.

EPISODE_NARRATIVE: A ridge of high pressure over the region for the last few weeks was nudged east by a potent upper-level trough coming off the Eastern Pacific.  The trough took on a strong negative tilt and passed through during peak heating.  An east to west line of strong thunderstorms developed over Northern Oregon and tracked northward through Eastern Washington and Northern Idaho during the late afternoon and early evening.  Additional storms continued to develop along the gust front which picked up momentum as it passed into the region into an inverted V environment.  The outflow winds picked up dust through Northern Oregon and Southern Washington resulting in a damaging dust storm or haboob.  Visibility along Interstate 90 was reduced below a quarter of a mile and numerous locations clocked winds between 50 and 60 mph resulting in isolated downed trees and power outages.  Proactive measures were taken at the Grant County Fair hours before the storm hit with nearly 8000 people evacuated and the fair cancelled to ensure the safety of the staff and attendees.  Reduced visibility from the dust led to multiple accidents in the Columbia Basin.
EVENT_NARRATIVE: Near zero visibility due to a haboob or dust storm contributed to a six vehicle accident along Interstate 90 twenty-two miles west of Ritzville.  Five people were injured in the accident.  A second accident was contributed to the dust involving two cars on Highway 395 twenty-six miles south of Ritzville.

Numerous tree limbs were down across the town of Palouse ranging between four and six inches in diameter.

EPISODE_NARRATIVE: The Watermelon Hill fire started in Lincoln County and was fanned by strong westerly winds east into Spokane County.  The fire was started by three individuals shooting explosive targets.
EVENT_NARRATIVE: The Watermelon Hill fire was wind fanned and quickly grew to 10,500 acres.  One hundred homes were threatened but no homes were destroyed in the fire.  Two hay barns were burned.  More than 100 residents had to be evacuated throughout the area including the Fish Trap Lake Resort.

EVENT_NARRATIVE: Route 153 was closed at Cow Creek due to a mudslide approximately one foot deep covering eighty feet of the roadway.  The slide crossed the road and went into the Twisp River.  Mud and rocks washed on to Route 20 near Loup Loup Pass from Frazer Creek.  A second hand report stated that a motorcyclist accident occurred at the location of the flow due to the motorcyclists attempting to drive through the mud.  It is unclear if the motorcyclist saw the mud or not.  It is unclear if the motorcyclist was injured.  French Creek at Burma Road flooded sending water and some debris onto private property and up to four homes.  At least two homes got some mud and water into home.

The storm weakened as it reached Brewster, Okanogan, and Omak.  These areas received wind gusts near 35 mph which blew down small branches causing some isolated power outages in the community of Twisp.

EPISODE_NARRATIVE: A closed upper-level low was positioned over Eastern Washington on the afternoon of July 14th.  The cold pool associated with the low combined with surface temperatures in the nineties led to a unstable afternoon air mass and widely scattered thunderstorms.  Precipitable water values were near nine tenths of an inch, the afternoon sounding indicated an inverted V profile, and the presence of the upper-low led to a low shear environment.  Nonetheless, isolated storms became strong producing a combination of heavy rain, small hail, and gusty outflow winds.  Thunderstorm winds knocked down trees in two small areas of Spokane and Okanogan Counties.
EVENT_NARRATIVE: Several trees including a seventy five foot healthy pine snapped.  Trees fell in the same direction to the west southwest.

EPISODE_NARRATIVE: The largest fire in Washington State history is now the 2014 Carlton Complex. Central Washington was in drought status. The fire was started by lightning on July 14. The complex started as four fires; the Stokes Fire, The Gold Hikes Fire, the French Creek Fire, and the Cougar Flat Fire. Explosive fire activity occurred on July 16th with very hot, dry, and unstable conditions in place. Each fire then made runs over the ridgetops and grew into one fire between July 17th and July 18th as hot and windy conditions developed. The fire rapidly spread through the communities of Methow, Pateros, Carlton, and Olema, and portions of Brewster consuming approximately 325 homes, barns, outbuildings and fences, utilities, cattle and livestock, and orchards, crops, forest, and range land in its path. The fire burned 269,186 acres consisting of a combination of brush, timber and heavy fuels.
EVENT_NARRATIVE: The lightning-caused fire, which began July 14, blackened 269,186 acres; leveled approximately 325 homes, 146 barns, outbuildings and fences; damaged utilities, killed cattle, wildlife and other livestock, and destroyed or damaged orchards, crops, forest and range land. Total cost to fight the fire was approximately 87 million dollars.  The fire spanned two different public zones and cost and acreage is highly uncertain per zone.

The winds also fanned new wildfire starts from lightning.  As the afternoon progressed, a combination of forcing from the shortwave and gust front continued to fuel additional storms over Northeastern Washington.   Outflow winds from these storms generally moved north to south.  As the north to south boundaries interacted with the westerly boundaries, some locations were spared experiencing wind gusts between 35 and 45 mph. EVENT_NARRATIVE: Trained spotter three miles southwest of Miles estimated winds between fifty and sixty miles per hour.  A secondary thunderstorm cell that developed along the gust front produced hail to the size of quarters briefly three miles southwest of Miles.

EVENT_NARRATIVE: Hail ranging between quarters and hen eggs fell across eastern reaches of Kettle Falls, WA.  Seven vehicles were damaged at Pingston Creek Road and Highway 395.  The vehicles were dented in spots and some windows were cracked.  The hail also left dents on metal roofs of shop buildings with one resident stating that it was the worst storm he witnessed in 72 years.  A resident two miles south southeast of Kettle Falls stated the hail destroyed the vinyl siding on the south and western portions of his home.  The storm continued to produce severe hail as it tracked northeast toward the Canadian Border.  Hail to the size of half dollars was reported by the public four miles east southeast of Northport, WA.  Winds estimated around 50 mph also accompanied the storm leading to some tree damage eight miles southeast of Northport, WA.

EPISODE_NARRATIVE: The largest fire in Washington State history is now the 2014 Carlton Complex. Central Washington was in drought status. The fire was started by lightning on July 14. The complex started as four fires; the Stokes Fire, The Gold Hikes Fire, the French Creek Fire, and the Cougar Flat Fire. Explosive fire activity occurred on July 16th with very hot, dry, and unstable conditions in place. Each fire then made runs over the ridgetops and grew into one fire between July 17th and July 18th as hot and windy conditions developed. The fire rapidly spread through the communities of Methow, Pateros, Carlton, and Olema, and portions of Brewster consuming approximately 325 homes, barns, outbuildings and fences, utilities, cattle and livestock, and orchards, crops, forest, and range land in its path. The fire burned 269,186 acres consisting of a combination of brush, timber and heavy fuels.
EVENT_NARRATIVE: The lightning-caused fire, which began July 14, blackened 269,186 acres; leveled approximately 325 homes, 146 barns, outbuildings and fences; damaged utilities, killed cattle, wildlife and other livestock, and destroyed or damaged orchards, crops, forest and range land. Total cost to fight the fire was approximately 87 million dollars.  The fire spanned two different public zones and cost and acreage is highly uncertain per zone.

EPISODE_NARRATIVE: A deep trough of low pressure dropped down the Washington Coastline then swung inland into the Inland Northwest during the afternoon of July 23rd.  The wave took on a strong negative tilt and moved into a moist and very unstable air mass.  The outcome was a widespread severe weather event for Eastern Washington and Northern Idaho producing large hail, flash flooding, and widespread wind damage.  Large hail and heavy rain were the initial threats early in the afternoon.  Late in the afternoon, the threat shifted to damaging winds with hundreds of downed or snapped trees.  The hardest hit areas were Northeastern Washington and Northern Idaho.  Falling trees led to some injuries in Eastern Washington.
EVENT_NARRATIVE: A wet microburst brought sustained winds of 35 to 50 mph with gusts 60 to 70 mph across Spokane County.  The winds descended near Cheney and peaked while passing through Airway Heights, Northwestern Spokane, and Deer Park.  Hundreds of Ponderosa Pines were snapped and downed from Northwestern Spokane to Deer Park.  Downed trees led to widespread structural damage, blocked roads, power outages, and injuries.  Significant damage was observed at the Riverside Village Mobile Home Park from trees falling on mobile homes prompting a search and rescue.  One woman was trapped but was not injured.  At least 40 mobile homes and cars in the park were severely damaged.  Forty eight trees were knocked down at a resort on Lake Eloika.  Trees fell on six RVs and the resort's oldest cabin forcing the owners to close their doors for a short period of time.  One person was injured when a tree landed on his vehicle while driving along Lakeside Dr near Reflection Lake in Elk, WA.  A tree branch flew through his windshield and drove him off the road severing his arm and impaling his hip.  He eventually lost his arm in the accident.  Three other people were injured near the Spokane Courthouse by falling tree branches.  Over 30,000 people lost power during the event.  Because of the extent of damage, some residents and businesses remained in the dark for up to four days.  Downed power poles and lightning strikes sparked several new wildfires which quickly grew due to the strong winds.  The winds completely damaged a airport hanger and caused between ten to twelve thousand dollars worth of damage to planes.

EPISODE_NARRATIVE: A strong upper-level ridge during the end of July and beginning August helped fuel a  moist and unstable air mass over the region.  The ridge broke down on the 1st of August and was overrun by two weak midlevel impulses on August 2nd.  As the second impulse passed over Central Washington during the late afternoon, strong to severe storms developed over the Waterville Plateau producing a combination of heavy rain and moderate size hail.  Meanwhile, dewpoint depressions over Eastern Washington had grown between 40 and 50 degrees Fahrenheit.  The rain and hail cooled outflow winds from storms over the Waterville Plateau spread east and gained momentum while tracking along the Highway 2 corridor across the Upper Columbia Basin.  Additional storms fired along the gust front and continued to reinforce the cold pool and resultant winds.  Gusty winds and blowing dust which originated over the Waterville Plateau traveled across Banks Lake and through much of Lincoln County but generally remained below severe speeds.  It wasn't until the outflow boundary moved into Eastern Lincoln County and Lake Roosevelt near Seven Bays Marina when wind speeds ranged between 60 and 70 miles per hour.  The winds led to numerous downed trees, waves four to six feet on Lake Roosevelt, widespread boat damage, power outages, and structural damage.  The winds also fanned new wildfire starts from lightning.  As the afternoon progressed, a combination of forcing from the shortwave and gust front continued to fuel additional storms over Northeastern Washington.   Outflow winds from these storms generally moved north to south.  As the north to south boundaries interacted with the westerly boundaries, some locations were spared experiencing wind gusts between 35 and 45 mph.  In this absence, winds of 50 to 70 mph were observed coupled with widespread tree damage.  A few storms briefly produced quarter size hail between Davenport and Northwest Spokane.
EVENT_NARRATIVE: The Midnight Mine RAWS measured a wind gust of 68 mph.  Numerous boats were damaged on Lake Roosevelt due to the winds.  Boaters and videos indicated wind waves of four to six feet on the Lake.  At least ten boats were severely damaged, two boats capsized, and numerous other boats received minor damage.  Several docks were damaged.  No injuries were reported.  Numerous trees were downed across southern Stevens County.

EVENT_NARRATIVE: A severe thunderstorm producing winds of 55 to 70 mph knocked down numerous trees across Pend Oreille County.  The storms also brought periods of very heavy rainfall and small hail.  The widespread tree damage led to power outages, blocked roads, and three injuries.  The worst of the damage occurred in southern areas of the county near Diamond and Sacheen Lakes.  There were two critical injuries at Diamond Lake.  A tree fell on a boat as a family was attempting to dock the boat.  The parents were critically injured but the two kids escaped without injury.

EVENT_NARRATIVE: Numerous trees were downed or snapped across Northern Spokane County including the communities of Deer Park, Chattaroy, Colbert, Mead, Nine Miles Falls, and Spokane north of Interstate 90 due to a west to east tracking outflow boundary.  A second cell developed along the boundary producing quarter size hail over Five Mile Prairie and sent an outflow boundary north to south across the South Hill and Spokane Valley roughly thirty minutes after the initial gust front went through.  A NWS employee stated winds associated with this boundary were less than 25 mph but witnessed a tree fall down.  Due to its isolated nature, it is assumed that this tree was damaged from a storm two weeks earlier.  Significant damage was observed from northwestern Spokane to Chattaroy to the Pend Oreille County border.  The Riverside Mobile Home Park was hit hard for a second time in as many weeks and forced to evacuate.  Numerous trees fell on homes.  One home on North Echo Road suffered major structural damage.  The nearby Eloika Lake Resort lost eight trees in the storm.  The trees damaged a home, RVs, and vehicle.  The resort which lost 48 trees in a storm ten days earlier was forced to shut down for the remainder of the summer.

EPISODE_NARRATIVE: A lightning strike on July 14th ignited a wildfire which burned through 4000 acres of wheat, sage brush, and grasses across Grant County east of Soap Lake.  The fire, ignited by lightning, originated near the intersection of Road H Northwest and Whitehall Road.
EVENT_NARRATIVE: Firefighters initially had trouble getting to the brush fire due to flooded roadways in the area from the same thunderstorm that ignited the blaze.

EPISODE_NARRATIVE: The Watermelon Hill fire started in Lincoln County and was fanned by strong westerly winds east into Spokane County.  The fire was started by three individuals shooting explosive targets.
EVENT_NARRATIVE: The Watermelon Hill fire was wind fanned and quickly grew to 10,500 acres. One hundred homes were threatened but no homes were destroyed in the fire. Two hay barns were burned. More than 100 residents had to be evacuated throughout the area including the Fish Trap Lake Resort.

EPISODE_NARRATIVE: More than 5,000 people in Woodinville and Redmond lost power after lightning strikes in the area.
EVENT_NARRATIVE: More than 5,000 people in Woodinville and Redmond lost power after lightning struck.

EPISODE_NARRATIVE: A warm front from the south collided with a cold arctic air mass from the north.  Significant moisture was transported across most of Oregon and the very southern portion of Washington.  High snow to liquid ratios allowed for 15 inches of snow to fall ENE of White Salmon and 4 inches SE of BZ Corner.   Most of Southern WA was missed by the storm due to the quicker intrusion of the dry arctic air.

A moist southerly flow overran cold air trapped against the Cascade Mountains and brought heavy snow accumulations to portions of North Central Washington. At the lowest elevation near the Columbia river 2 to 4 inches of snow was common. On the higher terrain near the river 4 to 6 inches occurred. In the Cascades 7 to 13 inches of snow was reported.
EVENT_NARRATIVE: Holden Village reported 12.6 inches of snow.

EPISODE_NARRATIVE: A broad upper level trough was established over the Pacific Northwest on the afternoon of September 30th.  An embedded shortwave dropping into the backside of the trough provided the trigger for showers and a few thunderstorms across north central Washington during the early afternoon hours.  One collapsing shower, which never produced lightning, produced outflow wind gusts near 60 mph leading to localized damage in the community of Oroville.
EVENT_NARRATIVE: The Oroville RAWS measured winds sustained at 37 mph with a gust to 61 mph between 1240 and 1340 LST.  The wind gust was associated with outflow from a decaying shower.  No lightning was observed with the convective shower.  The Oroville COOP reported damage to the power station.

EPISODE_NARRATIVE: A cold upper trough with vertical wind shear produced thunderstorms across Southwest Washington and Northwest Oregon. A line of intense thunderstorms was moving through the Portland Metro Area at about the same time a shower intensified over Kelso, Oregon and Longview, Washington. This thunderstorm produced tornado that touched down in Longview, Washington in the early afternoon resulting in around 1.3 miles of wind damage. A NWS Storm survey determined the damage to be consistent with an F1 tornado.
EVENT_NARRATIVE: Law enforcement confirmed that a tornado touched down near the Safeway in Longview, WA around 1240 PM. The tornado then traveled down Vandercook Way before dissipating near Main St. There was significant roof damage to industrial buildings, minor roof damage to residential buildings, 35 to 30 large trees snapped off or blew down, some power poles downed, and automobiles damaged from debris.

EPISODE_NARRATIVE: A 983mb low pressure system approached from the Southwest and moved north along the Central Oregon Coast before making landfall near Gray's Harbor, Washington. This low resulted in Gusty winds for the coast and coast range as well as damaging winds across Clark County.
EVENT_NARRATIVE: The Port of Vancouver measured a peak gust of 70 mph at around the same time wind damage was reported across the Vancouver Metro Area.  One large Maple tree was snapped in Brush Prairie while another one went down near Fort Vancouver resulting in minor damage to a historical building that is now Grant's Restaurant. A person from the public reported that a 150 pound piece of a pig pen was picked up by the wind and tossed through a double-paned glass window. Clark Public Utilities reported 4,500 homes experienced electricity outages.

EPISODE_NARRATIVE: The region was under a transitioning weather regime through the first week of March.  March 1st came in dry and very cold under a modified Arctic air mass.  This kept soil temperatures below freezing across much of the northeast portion of Washington.  March 2nd into the 3rd brought heavy snow across these same areas as dry northerly flow was replaced by milder and wetter southerly flow.  This wetter and milder flow regime continued into March 5th, but with snow levels lifting above valleys floors.  A pair of Pacific storm systems bringing through more sub-tropical moisture into the region resulted in moderate to heavy rainfall in the valleys.  The first weather system during the early morning hours on March 5th resulted in a short duration mixed precipitation event for portions of the Northeast Mountains.  This lead to pockets of icy roads early in the morning before temperatures warmed up late in the morning and in the afternoon.  The main impact over this wet period was flooding over the Palouse, upper potions of the Columbia Basin and in the Spokane Area.  Flooding was exasperated by low elevation snow melt and frozen soils.
EVENT_NARRATIVE: Heavy rain and low elevation snow melt led to standing water in low lying areas, flooding along small streams and creeks, road washouts and isolated reports of some basement flooding.  Spangle Creek flowed out of its banks resulting in water surrounding homes with basement flooding along a three block stretch.  A vehicle became stuck on a flooded road and had to be saved by rescue personnel.  There were reports of flooding in fields in Cheney and in Elk.  State Route 231 between milepost 0 and 28 was closed due to water and mud over the roadway.  Flood waters over Jackson Road took out an eight foot culvert and a portion of the road above the culvert.  A small creek near Shady Slope and Little Spokane Drive came out of its banks resulting in minor flooding.  Damage costs for the event were estimated.

EPISODE_NARRATIVE: The region was under a cold and dry air mass as the calendar flipped over to March 1st with an Arctic boundary pushing south of eastern Washington.  Drier northerly flow flipped around to a moister and mild southerly flow by March 2nd as a sub-tropical plume of moisture impacted the region.  Precipitation fell as snow over all valley locations before transitioning to a wintry mix and eventually rain from south to north as the cold Arctic air slowly eroded away.  Orographics played a big role as to where the heaviest snowfall occurred, including along the east slopes of the northern Cascades and in the Northeast Mountains.  Good isentropic ascent and colder temperatures also led to heavy snowfall across the Spokane Area.

Numerous areas across the Idaho Panhandle dealt with flooding during the second week of February. While minor flooding is a common occurrence during the late winter months when warmer temperatures, wind, and rain fall on valley snowpack, there was one ingredient of this event that made it unique and rather intense for many locations. This was the intrusion of two Arctic air masses; one in December and one in February. These bitterly cold air masses arrived in the absence of valley snow pack and froze the ground hard and deep. Shortly after the second Arctic air mass, snow began falling across the region with anywhere between six and twelve inches of valley snowfall. Then came an atmospheric river. This drove temperatures and dewpoints above freezing, led to windy conditions, and brought moderate to heavy rainfall. Nearly all the valley snowpack melted in a span of 24 hours.  Due to the rock hard ground, rain and snow melt could not percolate into the frozen ground equating to nearly 100 percent runoff.  This led to big problems in hilly terrain, low spots, and areas susceptible to poor drainage.  Some school districts were forced to delay school because buses were unable to navigate muddy or flooded back roads.
EVENT_NARRATIVE: Numerous areas of northern and central Whitman County experienced flooding between the 12th and 14th.  State route 23 was closed between Sprague and Saint John between mileposts 15 and 43 due to water over the roadway in several locations.    Colfax, WA experienced heavy flooding closing Mill Street from Canyon to Spring which also included mileposts 0 to 1 of SR 272.  Flood waters up to a foot were observed in portions of downtown Colfax.  Water undercut part of the roadway on SR27 five miles south of Oakesdale washing out part of the northbound lane.  Several homes in the community of Ewan were inundated with up to one foot of water.

A front combined with a coastal barrier jet produced strong winds for the Southwest Washington Coast on March 8, 2014. The NWS Doppler Radar Velocity product detected 50 to 60 kt winds just offshore the coast between 1500 and 4000 feet above the surface.
EVENT_NARRATIVE: The Oregon Department of Transportation's weather station on Megler Bridge measured sustained winds of 48 mph with a peak gust of 63 mph for 6 consecutive hours. Sustained winds of 49 mph were also reported at Cape Disappointment for 3 consecutive hours with a peak gust of 59 mph.

EPISODE_NARRATIVE: An Arctic front pushed into eastern Washington during the afternoon hours on February 28th.  The modified Arctic air mass intruding into the region out of Canada set up a strong pressure gradient across the area during the overnight hours into March 1st.  This set up strong northeast winds across the Spokane Area, out over the Upper Columbia Basin and into the Moses Lake Area.
EVENT_NARRATIVE: Power lines knocked down by toppled trees.  An estimated 5,000 customers lost power in the Spokane area.  Damage costs were estimated.

EVENT_NARRATIVE: Numerous areas across Spokane County experienced flooding on the 12th of February.  Due to frozen grounds, the flood problems persisted through the 14th in some cases.  A small ice jam on Coulee Heights Creek caused the creek to become diverted and flow into a private residence.  Water was approximately five feet deep in the garage and surrounding home.  A NWS employee observed up to two inches of water with some mud and small  debris flowing down Rambo Road just north of the Forecast Office.  Water was over a foot deep along the shoulders.  The swift water washed out portions of the shoulder and foundation to the edges of the pavement.  A few blocks away from the Forecast Office, water flooded the West Prairie Village Mobile home park located on the corner of Craig and Haid Lane.  It was estimated that one to two feet of water filled sections of the park.  In southern sections of the county, many secondary roads were flooded in low crossings.  State route 27 just north of Latah near milepost 58 was impassible for a period of time due to water over the road.  Water was also over the roadway along SR 2 two miles west of Airway Heights.  Damage extended into the Spokane Valley with damage or flooding observed near the intersection of Sanson and MacDonald, Crown Street at Forker Draw, Ridgemont Estates on Shamrock Street, and Thorpe Road at Chester Creek.

EPISODE_NARRATIVE: An Arctic front pushed into eastern Washington during the afternoon hours on February 28th.  The modified Arctic air mass intruding into the region out of Canada set up a strong pressure gradient across the area during the overnight hours into March 1st.  This set up strong northeast winds across the Spokane Area, out over the Upper Columbia Basin and into the Moses Lake Area.
EVENT_NARRATIVE: Strong winds blew off a tin roof in Grant County.  Damage costs were estimated.

EPISODE_NARRATIVE: An upper level low pressure system in the Gulf of Alaska late on March 8th into March 9th directed a slow north to south sagging atmospheric river across the area. The origins of this atmospheric river were from off of the Islands of Hawaii. This resulted in abnormally high moisture content for early to mid-March. This was a very mild weather pattern with precipitation falling as rain even over the highest mountain peaks.  Moderate to heavy rain was reported in the Northeast Mountains, especially for areas closer to the Idaho border.
EVENT_NARRATIVE: The southbound lane of State Route 31 at milepost 11 was blocked by a combination of mud and large rocks.  Time of the debris flow was estimated.

EPISODE_NARRATIVE: A vigorous occluded front pushed across the region through the early afternoon hours.  Steepening low to mid-level lapse rates resulted in a destabilizing atmosphere for the afternoon due to warming at the surface from the sun and cooling aloft by the approaching upper level shortwave trough.  Forcing along the upper level wave produced a squall line that stretched across the Palouse up into the Spokane Area.  Damaging winds were produced along the squall line up to the Idaho border.

EPISODE_NARRATIVE: The region was under a transitioning weather regime through the first week of March.  March 1st came in dry and very cold under a modified Arctic air mass.  This kept soil temperatures below freezing across much of the northeast portion of Washington.  March 2nd into the 3rd brought heavy snow across these same areas as dry northerly flow was replaced by milder and wetter southerly flow.  This wetter and milder flow regime continued into March 5th, but with snow levels lifting above valleys floors.  A pair of Pacific storm systems bringing through more sub-tropical moisture into the region resulted in moderate to heavy rainfall in the valleys.  The first weather system during the early morning hours on March 5th resulted in a short duration mixed precipitation event for portions of the Northeast Mountains.  This lead to pockets of icy roads early in the morning before temperatures warmed up late in the morning and in the afternoon.  The main impact over this wet period was flooding over the Palouse, upper potions of the Columbia Basin and in the Spokane Area.  Flooding was exasperated by low elevation snow melt and frozen soils.
EVENT_NARRATIVE: Bus carrying school children slid of the road and flipped on its side in Springdale.  The accident occurred on Cementery Road near Highway 231.  There were twenty-seven children on board the bus and eight of the children were transported to the hospital with injuries.

EPISODE_NARRATIVE: A relatively dry October 2013 through early February 2014 period with precipitation running around only 50 percent of normal turned unusually wet one week into February and continued through March. Precipitation during February and March ranked in the top 10 all-time for this time period, running between 150 and 200 percent of average. ||On a warm and dry 22nd of March, 2014, a large landslide occurred four miles east of the town of Oso, Washington. The slide debris, traveling at over 60 miles per hour, crossed the entire river valley, covering an area of over a square mile with an approximate deposit thickness of 15 to 75 feet in some areas. The force of the slide flow caused 43 fatalities, 12 injuries, destroyed 37 homes, and wiped out SR 530 for over a mile. In addition, the slide debris completely blocked the North Fork Stillaguamish River for over 24 hours, backing up a pool of water that flooded the valley about 2 miles upstream and reached approximately 20 feet deep, inundating an additional 6 homes. The cause of the slide is not fully known, though precipitation in the previous months to years contributed to the ground failure. A rain gage located 2 miles west of the slide had 19.93 inches of rain in the 40 days leading up to the slide. |As of March 19th the nearest reporting station Arlington, had reported 7.14 inches of rain, which is about 2 inches shy of the record 9.23 for the entire month of March.  Three days before the slide, the area received close to an inch of rain in one day.
EVENT_NARRATIVE: On a warm and dry 22nd of March, 2014, a large landslide occurred four miles east of the town of Oso, Washington. The slide debris, traveling at over 60 miles per hour, crossed the entire river valley, covering an area of over a square mile with an approximate deposit thickness of 15 to 75 feet in some areas. The force of the slide flow caused 43 fatalities, 12 injuries, destroyed 37 homes, and wiped out SR 530 for over a mile. In addition, the slide debris completely blocked the North Fork Stillaguamish River for over 24 hours, backing up a pool of water that flooded the valley about 2 miles upstream and reached approximately 20 feet deep, inundating an additional 6 homes. The cause of the slide is not fully known, though precipitation in the previous months to years contributed to the ground failure. A rain gage located 2 miles west of the slide had 19.93 inches of rain in the 40 days leading up to the slide. |As of March 19th the nearest reporting station Arlington, had reported 7.14 inches of rain, which is about 2 inches shy of the record 9.23 for the entire month of March.  Three days before the slide, the area received close to an inch of rain in one day. Most all of the fatalities occurred at the permanent homes impacted by the slide.

EVENT_NARRATIVE: The City of Portland issued its first Wireless Emergency Alert in history on March 9th at 8 AM warning people to stay indoors due to dangerous ice conditions. While most of the icing has stopped by then, there was still significant ice loading on branches and power lines as well as several of each downed. Power outages continued into the 9th due to the ice, but ice slowly melted as temperatures crept above freezing. Some ice accumulations reported from spotters include a 0.25 inch in Camas, and a 0.25 inch of ice in Battle Ground.

EPISODE_NARRATIVE: A strong upper-level ridge during the end of July and beginning August helped fuel a  moist and unstable air mass over the region.  The ridge broke down on the 1st of August and was overrun by two weak midlevel impulses on August 2nd.  As the second impulse passed over Central Washington during the late afternoon, strong to severe storms developed over the Waterville Plateau producing a combination of heavy rain and moderate size hail.  Meanwhile, dewpoint depressions over Eastern Washington had grown between 40 and 50 degrees Fahrenheit.  The rain and hail cooled outflow winds from storms over the Waterville Plateau spread east and gained momentum while tracking along the Highway 2 corridor across the Upper Columbia Basin.  Additional storms fired along the gust front and continued to reinforce the cold pool and resultant winds.  Gusty winds and blowing dust which originated over the Waterville Plateau traveled across Banks Lake and through much of Lincoln County but generally remained below severe speeds.  It wasn't until the outflow boundary moved into Eastern Lincoln County and Lake Roosevelt near Seven Bays Marina when wind speeds ranged between 60 and 70 miles per hour.  The winds led to numerous downed trees, waves four to six feet on Lake Roosevelt, widespread boat damage, power outages, and structural damage.  The winds also fanned new wildfire starts from lightning.  As the afternoon progressed, a combination of forcing from the shortwave and gust front continued to fuel additional storms over Northeastern Washington.   Outflow winds from these storms generally moved north to south.  As the north to south boundaries interacted with the westerly boundaries, some locations were spared experiencing wind gusts between 35 and 45 mph.  In this absence, winds of 50 to 70 mph were observed coupled with widespread tree damage.  A few storms briefly produced quarter size hail between Davenport and Northwest Spokane.
EVENT_NARRATIVE: Trees and power lines were blown down from thunderstorm winds across portions of Nespelem.

EPISODE_NARRATIVE: A closed upper-level low over the Washington Cascades delivered a low shear but unstable environment.  Sufficient atmospheric moisture, characterized by precipitable water values near one inch, was already in place.  A combination of afternoon heating and lift along spokes of energy pivoting around the main low sparked numerous rounds of wet thunderstorms between the early morning hours of August 13th through morning of the 15th.  The heaviest storms were delivering around one inch of precipitation over a thirty to forty minute time period.  Several debris flows were observed in areas where the storms moved over burn scars.
EVENT_NARRATIVE: Water was flowing over the roadway half a mile east of Creston on Miles Creston Road.

EPISODE_NARRATIVE: An upper level ridge combined with a surface thermal trough and low level offshore winds resulted in a hot day across Southwest Washington where inland temperatures peaked in the upper 90s inland and the upper 80s along the coast. The NWS Portland office had a heat advisory in effect. Many people flocked to the rivers to cool from the heat and there were unfortunately a drowning. Another man was hospitalized after rescuing a woman and two children from the Toutle River.
EVENT_NARRATIVE: An injury from swimming in the Toutle River, WA during a hot weather spell could be indirectly related to the hot temperatures on July 1, 2014.  A man and two children were swimming in the Toutle River when a strong current took the three of them under water. The girl's mother jumped in to help, and also got caught in the current. The man was able to get the woman, her two daughters, and himself to shore and was later airlifted to the hospital for injuries received during the process.

EVENT_NARRATIVE: A drowning in the Lewis River near Yacolt, WA is indirectly related to the hot temperatures as the victim likely went to the river to gain relief from the heat . The victim was a 44 year old man that drowned after jumping from the rocks at Moulton Falls.

EPISODE_NARRATIVE: A 600 acre wildfire broke out around 1330 PST on August 1st west of Highway 20, south of Winthrop near Rising Eagle Road.  The fire's cause is still under investigation.  Firefighters were able to secure a containment line by August 3rd with mop up operations through the 5th of August.  This fire was not part of the Carlton Complex.
EVENT_NARRATIVE: The Rising Eagle Road Wildfire threatened fifty homes and one home was lost in the blaze.

EPISODE_NARRATIVE: The Chiwaukum Complex was seven fires spread across Chelan County between Lake Chelan and the Highway 2 corridor. The complex consisted of the Chiwaukum Creek, Shoofly, Mills Canyon, Kelly Mountain, Alpine Fire, Duncan, and Hansel Creek. The fires erupted on July 15th at approximately 0600 PST from lightning on July 14th. Hot and dry conditions on the 16th followed by hot and windy conditions on the 17th and 18th helped the fires spread rapidly. Steep terrain in the East Slopes of the Cascades also contributed to wildfire growth. The fire burned through a combination of timber including Ponderosa pine, grand fir, silver fir, mountain hemlock, sub-alpine fir, and slash.
EVENT_NARRATIVE: The Chiwaukum Complex started from a lightning strike on July 14th but did not erupt until July 15th at approximately 0600 PST. The complex of fires burned 50,540 acres. 543 homes were threatened over the 45 day period but none were lost. Four other structures were burned including one outbuilding and three seasonal cabins. The approximate cost for fighting the fire was 38 million dollars. The Chiwaukum Creek Fire burned 13,895 acres; Alpine Fire burned 19 acres; Kelly Mountain Fire burned 124 acres; Shoofly Fire burned 163 acres; Mills Canyon Fire burned 22,571 acres; Hansel Creek Fire burned 1,073 acres; Duncan Fire burned 12,695 acres north of Entiat, WA.

Hot and dry conditions on the 16th followed by hot and windy conditions on the 17th and 18th helped the fires spread rapidly.  Steep terrain in the East Slopes of the Cascades also contributed to wildfire growth.  The fire burned through a combination of timber including Ponderosa pine, grand fir, silver fir, mountain hemlock, sub-alpine fir, and slash.
EVENT_NARRATIVE: The Chiwaukum Complex erupted on July 15th at approximately 0600 PST and was started from a lightning strike on July 14th.  The complex of fires burned 50,540 acres. 543 homes were threatened over the 45 day period but none were lost. Four other structures were burned including one outbuilding and three seasonal cabins. The approximate cost for fighting the fire was 38 million dollars.  The Chiwaukum Creek Fire burned 13,895 acres; Alpine Fire burned 19 acres; Kelly Mountain Fire burned 124 acres; Shoofly Fire burned 163 acres; Mills Canyon Fire burned 22,571 acres; Hansel Creek Fire burned 1,073 acres; Duncan Fire burned 12,695 acres north of Entiat, WA.

EPISODE_NARRATIVE: A deep trough of low pressure dropped down the Washington Coastline then swung inland into the Inland Northwest during the afternoon of July 23rd.  The wave took on a strong negative tilt and moved into a moist and very unstable air mass.  The outcome was a widespread severe weather event for Eastern Washington and Northern Idaho producing large hail, flash flooding, and widespread wind damage.  Large hail and heavy rain were the initial threats early in the afternoon.  Late in the afternoon, the threat shifted to damaging winds with hundreds of downed or snapped trees.  The hardest hit areas were Northeastern Washington and Northern Idaho.  Falling trees led to some injuries in Eastern Washington.
EVENT_NARRATIVE: The Alpowa Summit observation platform located along Highway 12 at 1775 feet clocked sustained winds of 48 mph with gusts to 60 mph.

EPISODE_NARRATIVE: An upper level low pressure system off the coast of southwest Oregon slowly moved inland the afternoon and evening of August 13th.  This lead to another round of showers and thunderstorms for most of the forecast area. Heavy rain and large hail were the main severe weather threats on this day.  But an isolated tornado was reported near Rattle Snake Mountain.

EVENT_NARRATIVE: Small tornado touched down in an open field with no damage reported.

A broad upper-level low was over the region on August 22nd.  In addition to providing the instability, the weak winds aloft within the center of the low resulted in very little to no storm motion.  Precipitable water values ranged between 0.80 and 0.90 inches.  Widely scattered showers developed the afternoon of the 22nd.  As these storms moved across 2014 burn scars, the heavy rainfall and hydrophobic soils resulted in debris flows.  Failure of natural dams within Finley Canyon created a floodwave down Bensen Creek Road which trapped several vehicles and washed out roadways.
EVENT_NARRATIVE: A debris flow from a thunderstorm closed Highway 97 north of Orondo.

EPISODE_NARRATIVE: A low pressure system off the coast moved across Oregon and Washington with showers and thunderstorms. The thunderstorms started off in central Oregon and tracked northward through the day. Good dynamics with the low and associated jet allowed for several strong to severe thunderstorms to develop. This in turn would become a line of thunderstorms with damaging outflow winds by the time it reached the Lower Columbia Basin. Visibility was reduced to a quarter of a mile in several places due to blowing dust. The highest wind gust reported was 68 MPH near Hanford.

EVENT_NARRATIVE: At approximately 1745 PST, a debris flow blocked Benson Creek Road six miles south of Twisp.  Benson Creek eventually flowed across Highway 153 blocking the road at milepost 24.  An hour later, at 1845 PST, a three to four foot flood wave came down Benson Creek Road trapping several vehicles.  One vehicle which was transporting a convoy of resources from the Carlton Complex was carried down the road by the torrent of water approximately 1000 feet until it got caught in a log jam.  No passengers were injured.  Some people had to be rescued from their vehicles.  Debris flows from earlier rains plugged spillways along five man made lakes known as the Wenner Lakes.  Due to the plugged spillways, three of the five natural dams holding back the Wenner Lakes breached sending a floodwave down Benson Creek Road.  No injuries were reported.  Five to ten vehicles were cut off by the mud and slides and were temporarily unable to get off Highway 153 in the Methow Valley.  The Chiliwist Area was under mandatory evacuations due to the flooding and deteriorating road conditions.  Highway 20 closed due to multiple debris flows between B-O Road and intersection of Highway 153 between mile posts 204 and 229.  The DOT reported that the road was partially washed away in places and unsafe for travel.  The Incident Command Post in Winthrop had significant flood damage to some yurts and some fire personnel had to be relocated.
