EVENT_NARRATIVE: Thunderstorm winds blew down trees across several areas of Ferry County.  Downed trees and power lines were reported in Keller, Inchelium, and Danville.  A media picture from the county shows damage to a car port but the exact location is unknown.

EPISODE_NARRATIVE: A strong Pacific jet nosing into central Oregon from the northwest drove a cold upper-level low from Canada into the Inland Northwest.  Southwest flow ahead of the trough resulted in a period of moist isentropic ascent across southeastern Washington bringing the first round of light snow.  Snow intensified overnight on the 6th as a surface low deepened over northeastern Oregon setting up a narrow deformation band of moderate to heavy snow.  Many locations across southeastern Washington received between one and three inches of snow but upwards of four to six inches fell within the deformation and across the Washington Palouse.
EVENT_NARRATIVE: Snowfall amounts ranged from three to five inches leading to hazardous travel during the Friday morning commute.

EPISODE_NARRATIVE: A moist warm front overrunning an established pool of cold air over Eastern Washington resulted in several hours of light to moderate snowfall on the 10th of February.  The heaviest snow accumulations were found in the East Slopes of the Washington Cascades which received between seven to fourteen inches.  The remainder of the region saw snowfall amounts generally under two inches.  The exception was along a narrow corridor across Northeastern Washington, generally south of Pend Oreille River and north of the Palouse, extending east to west from Lincoln County to the Idaho Montana border.  A nearly stationary boundary coupled with the rising terrain resulted in several hours of moderate snow which accumulated between three and four inches over a span of just a few hours.
EVENT_NARRATIVE: The heaviest snow started around 0800 PST with storm total amounts in the three to four inch range across the Spokane Area.

EPISODE_NARRATIVE: Periods of moderate to heavy snow continued for the seventh straight day across the East Slopes of the Washington Cascades. A persistent westerly jet continued to feed subtropical moisture along just over the Cascades Crest.  Over a foot of snow fell along and adjacent to the crest impacting the communities of Plain, Holden Village, and Mazama. Snowfall amounts near ten inches were common away from the crest in locations like Leavenworth.
EVENT_NARRATIVE: Heavy snow was confined to elevations of 3500 feet and higher.  Local ski resorts reported seven to nine inches of snow from the event.  In the valleys up to three inches of snow fell before switching over to rain.

EPISODE_NARRATIVE: Periods of moderate to heavy snow continued for the seventh straight day across the East Slopes of the Washington Cascades. A persistent westerly jet continued to feed subtropical moisture along just over the Cascades Crest.  Over a foot of snow fell along and adjacent to the crest impacting the communities of Plain, Holden Village, and Mazama. Snowfall amounts near ten inches were common away from the crest in locations like Leavenworth.
EVENT_NARRATIVE: Heavy snow fell at elevations of 2000 feet and higher with the exception of Stehekin, WA which is near the Cascade Crest.  Snowfall amounts at Plain, Leavenworth, and Stehekin were generally in the eight to fourteen inch range.  The most impressive twenty-four hour total was received from a COOP at Holden Village which reported seventeen inches.  This was the seventh snow storm to impact the East Slopes of the Washington Cascades since the 8th of February.  A family of six became stranded in a backcountry cabin located in the Sears Creek drainage.  The family had skied into the cabin on the 15th and were unable to ski out because two to three feet of new snow had fallen in the four day period.  Chelan County Search and Rescue successfully rescued the family utilizing a snow cat.

EPISODE_NARRATIVE: Conditions on the 20th of February were generally mild for February standards with periods of light rain.  By the evening and overnight hours, skies cleared and temperatures cooled just near the freezing mark.  In addition, Arctic air mass intrusions during the last two months had led to a deep hard freeze of the subsurface.  These conditions were thought to be responsible for patches of ice along sections of US 195 across the Washington Palouse during the early morning hours of February 21st.  A car carrying four passengers lost control near mile post 36 crossing into the southbound lane and running head on into a semi-truck.  Two passengers were killed in the accident and two other were injured.
EVENT_NARRATIVE: Two passengers were killed and two others were injured when a car lost control on icy roads and ran head on into a semi-truck.  One passenger was ejected from the car.

EPISODE_NARRATIVE: Numerous areas across the Idaho Panhandle dealt with flooding during the second week of February. While minor flooding is a common occurrence during the late winter months when warmer temperatures, wind, and rain fall on valley snowpack, there was one ingredient of this event that made it unique and rather intense for many locations. This was the intrusion of two Arctic air masses; one in December and one in February. These bitterly cold air masses arrived in the absence of valley snow pack and froze the ground hard and deep. Shortly after the second Arctic air mass, snow began falling across the region with anywhere between six and twelve inches of valley snowfall. Then came an atmospheric river. This drove temperatures and dewpoints above freezing, led to windy conditions, and brought moderate to heavy rainfall. Nearly all the valley snowpack melted in a span of 24 hours.  Due to the rock hard ground, rain and snow melt could not percolate into the frozen ground equating to nearly 100 percent runoff.  This led to big problems in hilly terrain, low spots, and areas susceptible to poor drainage.  Some school districts were forced to delay school because buses were unable to navigate muddy or flooded back roads.
EVENT_NARRATIVE: Areas of flooding were observed across portions of northern and eastern Adams County.  State route 21 nine miles south of Odessa was flooded for a period of time.

EVENT_NARRATIVE: Numerous areas across Lincoln County dealt with flooding between the 12th and 14th.  Some sections of highways that were flooded include SR 23 between mileposts 57 and 60 south of Harrington, SR 231 between mileposts 36 and 42 near Reardon, SR 231 between mileposts 18 and 19 near Edwall, SR 25 between Davenport and Lake Roosevelt, and SR 21 between mileposts 57 and 60 near Harrington.  A creek that runs through the middle of the Edwall flooded the Post Office and adjacent businesses with one to two feet of water.  Edwall-Reardon school district was forced to delay school openings because buses were struggling to navigate muddy dirt roads.  A tech company located on SR 231 reported 30 inches of water flooded its manufacturing facility.  Fortunately, the staff was able to get all valuable equipment off the floor before the flood waters entered.

EPISODE_NARRATIVE: Conditions on the 12th of February were generally mild for February standards with periods of light rain and temperatures in the forties.  By the evening and overnight hours, skies cleared and temperatures cooled into the thirties.  In addition, Arctic air mass intrusions during the last two months had led to a deep hard freeze of the subsurface. These conditions were thought to be responsible for patches of ice along sections of US 195, US 2, and Spokane Area during the morning commute of February 13th.  Numerous accidents, some containing injuries were reported across the area.  Between 1100 PST and 1400 PST, strong winds sustained between 20 and 30 miles per hour along with gusts estimated around 45 miles per hour impacted the Spokane Area snapping a thirty five foot section of a pine tree.  The tree limb fell into a Bengal tiger enclosure at Cat Tales Zoo in Mead, WA.
EVENT_NARRATIVE: Icy roadways across the Spokane Area led to multiple accident during the morning commute.  The largest accidents occurred along Highway 2 near Spotted Road and State  Route 195 near Mullen Hill Road.

EPISODE_NARRATIVE: Rain from a series of fronts resulted in flooding for the Grays river at Rosburg.
EVENT_NARRATIVE: The Grays river at Rosburg reached flood stage at 150 AM PST. The river level crested at 13.2 feet at 545 AM PST, and fell below flood stage at 100 PM PST.

EVENT_NARRATIVE: A large tree branch snapped off a tree and landed in a Bengal tiger enclosure at Cat Tales in Mead, WA.  The 35 foot section of pine tree snapped a guide wire and crashed through two fences that hold the big cats.  No tigers or people were injured during the event.

EPISODE_NARRATIVE: A complex weather pattern across the Eastern Pacific led to region-wide light to moderate snow.  A closed upper-level high was positioned over southeastern Alaska.  The high steered modified Arctic air southward into the Inland Northwest.  Meanwhile, a moist westerly jet undercut the Alaskan high bringing a source of moisture into the region.  A combination of a stalled frontal boundary, topography, and isentropic processes resulted in region-wide snow.  The heaviest snow was delivered in two main waves; the first occurred from the evening hours of the 22nd through the evening of the 23rd with a second surge of moderate to heavy snow during the day on the 24th.  The heaviest snow accumulations were concentrated across the East Slopes of the Washington Cascades and Central Panhandle Mountains of Idaho which received between one to three feet of snow.  Nearly all other valley locations received between four to ten inches over the multi-day period.
EVENT_NARRATIVE: Snowfall amounts across the Spokane Area ranged between four and six inches from heavy snow on the 24th.  The previous day, the same area received between two and four inches resulting in two day totals of six to nine inches.

EVENT_NARRATIVE: The East Slopes of the Washington Cascades experienced nearly three days of light to moderate snow and occasionally periods of heavy snow from February 22nd through the morning hours of the 25th.  Snowfall totals over the three day period ranged from eight inches across the eastern reaches of Chelan County to forty inches along and near the Cascade Crest.  Mazama, located in the western reaches of the Methow Valley received seventeen inches of snow.

EVENT_NARRATIVE: Snowfall amounts on the 23rd were in the two to four inch range.  A second round of snow on the 24th brought an additional two inches leading to two day totals in the four to seven inch range.

EPISODE_NARRATIVE: An Arctic air mass spilled into the Inland Northwest between the 4th and 5th of February. The combination of single digit temperatures and winds on the order of 10 to 25 mph with gusts in excess of 30 mph created wind chills between minus fifteen and minus twenty-five degrees Fahrenheit in the valleys and up to minus thirty degrees Fahrenheit in the mountains during the morning hours on the 5th.   Bitterly cold wind chills continued during the morning of the 6th but were paired with slightly cooler temperatures and weaker winds.  The cold and bitterly cold wind chills led to school closures in the Ephrata School District.
EVENT_NARRATIVE: Wind chill values varied due to complex terrain and sheltering. Windier locations experienced wind chills between minus 15 and minus 20 degrees Fahrenheit. Wind chills in the sheltered valleys only dropped between minus 5 and minus 12 degrees Fahrenheit.  The Deer Park ASOS observed a wind chill of minus 20 degrees Fahrenheit.

EPISODE_NARRATIVE: An Arctic air mass spilled into the Inland Northwest between the 4th and 5th of February. The combination of single digit temperatures and winds on the order of 10 to 25 mph with gusts in excess of 30 mph created wind chills between minus fifteen and minus twenty-five degrees Fahrenheit in the valleys and up to minus thirty degrees Fahrenheit in the mountains during the morning hours on the 5th.   Bitterly cold wind chills continued during the morning of the 6th but were paired with slightly cooler temperatures and weaker winds.  The cold and bitterly cold wind chills led to school closures in the Ephrata School District.
EVENT_NARRATIVE: Very cold temperatures and winds near 10 mph resulted in wind chills of minus 10 to minus 21 across the zone.  The coldest reading of minus 21 was at the Teepee Seed Orchard RAWS.

EPISODE_NARRATIVE: A complex weather pattern across the Eastern Pacific led to region-wide light to moderate snow.  A closed upper-level high was positioned over southeastern Alaska.  The high steered modified Arctic air southward into the Inland Northwest.  Meanwhile, a moist westerly jet undercut the Alaskan high bringing a source of moisture into the region.  A combination of a stalled frontal boundary, topography, and isentropic processes resulted in region-wide snow.  The heaviest snow was delivered in two main waves; the first occurred from the evening hours of the 22nd through the evening of the 23rd with a second surge of moderate to heavy snow during the day on the 24th.  The heaviest snow accumulations were concentrated across the East Slopes of the Washington Cascades and Central Panhandle Mountains of Idaho which received between one to three feet of snow.  Nearly all other valley locations received between four to ten inches over the multi-day period.
EVENT_NARRATIVE: Valley snowfall amounts over the three day period ranged between seven and ten inches. Three to four inches of snow fell between the 22nd and morning of the 24th. An additional three to six inches fell on the 24th.

EPISODE_NARRATIVE: A strong Pacific jet with ties to subtropical moisture setup across central Oregon allowing an active storm track along the Washington, Oregon border.  The second low pressure system in as many days took aim on southeastern Washington during the overnight and morning hours of the 8th of February.  A band of light to moderate snow on the northeastern periphery brought one to three inches of snow across the Washington Palouse.  A third low pressure system during the evening of the 8th into the morning of the 9th dropped another three to four inches.  Meanwhile, northeast winds drawn into the system continued to bring modified arctic air from Canada into the region.  The combination of temperatures in the teens and northeast winds between 15 and 25 mph resulted in blowing snow and large drifts across the Palouse closing sections of Highway 27 on each the 8th and 9th of February.  Road closures on the 8th were centered between mile posts 59 and the community of Latah.  On the 9th, Highway 27 was closed between mile posts 48 and 63.
EVENT_NARRATIVE: Snowfall amounts ranged between three and four inches.

EVENT_NARRATIVE: Snowfall amounts from the 22nd to the 23rd were near two inches in northern Lincoln County to half an inch in Adams County.  A second round of snow on the 24th brought an additional two to three inches across southern reaches of the zone to four inches across northern Lincoln County.

EPISODE_NARRATIVE: A cold and conditionally unstable air mass had become established across the Inland Northwest as a cold upper-level trough anchored over the region.  A second low pressure system within a 36 hour period tracked across northern Oregon spreading a band of moderate snow within a deformation axis across extreme southeastern Washington and the lower Idaho Panhandle.  As the low pressure system departed to the east, northwest flow in its wake led to numerous snow showers across the rising terrain of the Blue Mountains.
EVENT_NARRATIVE: Snowfall amounts ranged between four and seven inches.

EVENT_NARRATIVE: One to three inches of snow fell across the Washington Palouse on the 8th, falling on top of two to four inches from the previous day.  Meanwhile, modified arctic air moving into the region brought gusty northeast winds resulting in blowing and drifting snow.  Large drifts resulted in a road closure on the 8th along a section of Highway 27 which stretches across southern Spokane and northern Whitman Counties.

EPISODE_NARRATIVE: Numerous areas across the Idaho Panhandle dealt with flooding during the second week of February. While minor flooding is a common occurrence during the late winter months when warmer temperatures, wind, and rain fall on valley snowpack, there was one ingredient of this event that made it unique and rather intense for many locations. This was the intrusion of two Arctic air masses; one in December and one in February. These bitterly cold air masses arrived in the absence of valley snow pack and froze the ground hard and deep. Shortly after the second Arctic air mass, snow began falling across the region with anywhere between six and twelve inches of valley snowfall. Then came an atmospheric river. This drove temperatures and dewpoints above freezing, led to windy conditions, and brought moderate to heavy rainfall. Nearly all the valley snowpack melted in a span of 24 hours.  Due to the rock hard ground, rain and snow melt could not percolate into the frozen ground equating to nearly 100 percent runoff.  This led to big problems in hilly terrain, low spots, and areas susceptible to poor drainage.  Some school districts were forced to delay school because buses were unable to navigate muddy or flooded back roads.
EVENT_NARRATIVE: A trained spotter located three miles northeast of Loon Lake reported two to three inches of standing water on property and around the area.  There were no known road closures from flooding.

EPISODE_NARRATIVE: Several landslides were reported in Western Washington as recent rains break loose steeper hillsides.  ||An unoccupied Pacific home was destroyed Wednesday morning after it was hit by a slide on West Valley Highway.  The force of the slide knocked down several walls of the house.||A mudslide on West Valley Highway in Auburn closed the road between 15th and 37th. |A landslide has closed Marine View Drive from East 11th Street to Slayden Road in Tacoma.
EVENT_NARRATIVE: An unoccupied Pacific home was destroyed after it was hit by a slide on West Valley Highway. The force of the slide knocked down several walls of the house and knocked the home off its foundation. A road was destroyed in Index (Sunset Falls). Other slide locations across roadways were in the City of Snoqualmie, Thurston County, West Valley Highway in Auburn, Marine View Drive from East 11th Street to Slayden Road in Tacoma, Vashon Island���s Dockton Road, on the narrow strip of land connecting Vashon and Maury islands, mudslide struck a car east of Sedro Woolley when mud and debris came down on the South Skagit Highway, Maxwell Road in Maple Valley slid out, Mulkilteo (2 slides), westbound lanes of Highway 20 east of Concrete, Mukilteo again, Des Moines rolled down into a parking lot at an apartment complex along 277th block 6th Place South; hit cars, southbound US 101 near Hoodsport.
